questions.question: Which ancient wonder was located in Alexandria, Egypt?
questions.explanation: The Lighthouse of Alexandria (Pharos) stood on the island of Pharos and guided sailors into the harbor. It was one of the tallest man-made structures of the ancient world.
questions.memory_hook: Pharos = lighthouse. Alexandria's pharaoh needed a light on.

questions.question: What year did the Berlin Wall fall?
questions.explanation: The Berlin Wall fell on November 9, 1989, after East Germany announced that its citizens could cross the border freely. It had divided East and West Berlin since 1961.
questions.memory_hook: '89 — think of it as the end of an era: 1-9-8-9, the Iron Curtain's last breath.

questions.question: Which tiny European republic, entirely surrounded by Italy and claiming to be founded in 301 AD, is considered the world's oldest surviving republic?
questions.explanation: San Marino claims to be the world's oldest republic, traditionally founded in 301 AD. Entirely surrounded by Italy, it sits atop Mount Titano and covers just 61 square kilometers. Unlike most tiny European states, it is a full republic — two Captains Regent serve simultaneously as co-heads of state for six-month terms, a system dating back to the 13th century. San Marino has avoided most European wars by maintaining strict neutrality.
questions.memory_hook: San Marino: oldest republic (301 AD), inside Italy on Mount Titano. Two Captains Regent rule together, each for six months. Tiny country, ancient democracy.

questions.question: How many rings are on the Olympic flag?
questions.explanation: The five interlocking rings on the Olympic flag represent the five continents of the world united by the Olympic movement. The colors — blue, yellow, black, green, and red — include at least one color from every national flag.
questions.memory_hook: Five fingers, five continents, five rings.

questions.question: In which sport would you perform a 'slam dunk'?
questions.explanation: A slam dunk occurs when a player jumps and forcefully pushes the ball through the basket from above the rim. It was officially allowed in college basketball in 1976 after being banned for nearly a decade.
questions.memory_hook: Slam: the sound of the ball hitting the board. Dunk: like dunking a cookie — just more athletic.

questions.question: What country has won the most FIFA World Cup titles?
questions.explanation: Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup five times (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002), more than any other nation. They are the only country to have played in every World Cup tournament.
questions.memory_hook: Brazil = 5 cups. Think of five fingers on a hand holding a trophy.

questions.question: Which film features the song 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow'?
questions.explanation: 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow' was performed by Judy Garland as Dorothy in the 1939 film The Wizard of Oz. It was voted the greatest song of the 20th century by the Recording Industry Association of America.
questions.memory_hook: Dorothy's over the rainbow, not in Kansas anymore.

questions.question: Who directed the film 'Schindler's List'?
questions.explanation: Steven Spielberg directed Schindler's List in 1993. The film won seven Academy Awards including Best Picture and Best Director, and is widely regarded as one of the greatest films ever made.
questions.memory_hook: Spielberg spelled out the horror — he also gave us Jaws, E.T., and Indiana Jones.

questions.question: Which band released the album 'Abbey Road'?
questions.explanation: Abbey Road was released by The Beatles in September 1969. The iconic cover photo of the four band members crossing Abbey Road became one of the most imitated images in music history.
questions.memory_hook: Abbey Road = Beatles crossing. Think of the four of them in a row.

questions.question: In chemistry, what is the name for a solution that resists significant changes to its pH when small amounts of acid or base are added?
questions.explanation: A buffer solution maintains a relatively stable pH when small amounts of acid or base are added. Buffers work because they contain a weak acid and its conjugate base, which absorb added ions. Blood is one of nature's most critical buffers, maintaining a pH between 7.35 and 7.45 — a shift of just 0.5 units in either direction can be life-threatening. Carbonic acid and bicarbonate ions keep blood pH stable.
questions.memory_hook: Buffer = pH stabilizer. Your blood is a buffer (pH 7.35-7.45). Shift too far in either direction and cells die. Simple chemistry keeps you alive.

questions.question: Which is the world's smallest country by area?
questions.explanation: Vatican City covers just 0.44 square kilometers, located entirely within Rome, Italy. It is the headquarters of the Roman Catholic Church and home to the Pope. Despite its tiny size, it has its own government, currency, passport, radio station, and newspaper.
questions.memory_hook: Vatican City = smallest country, inside a city. Smaller than most golf courses. An entire sovereign nation inside Rome.

questions.question: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.explanation: Au comes from 'Aurum,' the Latin word for gold. The periodic table uses Latin (and sometimes Greek) roots for many elements — that's why the symbols don't always match the English name. Silver is Ag (Argentum), iron is Fe (Ferrum), and lead is Pb (Plumbum).
questions.memory_hook: Gold = Au = Aurum. The Romans named it. The symbol stuck for 2,000 years.
